natural: Casual 'yeah / yep' to agree or acknowledge among equals or to someone younger; never say it up to an elder (use 'vâng').
whoUses: everyone, in casual speech with peers or juniors
usageContext: answering a friend, agreeing informally; 'ừ' (huyền) and 'ờ' are interchangeable, 'ờ' sounds a touch more offhand.

examples.text: Đi ăn không? - Ừ, đi.
examples.translation: Wanna go eat? - Yeah, let's go.

natural: 'Awesome / top-tier'; calling something the peak means it's excellent. Often stretched to 'đỉnh của chóp' (the peak of the peak) for extra praise.
usageContext: praising a performance, food, skill, or product online or in chat.

examples.text: Trận đấu hôm nay đỉnh thật!
examples.translation: Today's match was seriously awesome!

natural: 'Cool / classy / got quality'; describes a person, style, or thing with real character. Also heard as 'chất chơi' (stylishly cool).
usageContext: complimenting someone's style, music, or vibe.

examples.text: Áo này chất phết đấy.
examples.translation: This shirt is pretty cool.

natural: 'High-quality / the real deal / premium'; the opposite of fake or cheap. Intensified as 'xịn sò'.
usageContext: describing a good phone, brand-name goods, or anything top-notch.

examples.text: Điện thoại mới của cậu xịn quá.
examples.translation: Your new phone is really top-notch.

natural: 'Oh my god / oh gosh'; all-purpose exclamation of surprise, dismay, or exasperation.
usageContext: reacting to shocking, annoying, or overwhelming news.

examples.text: Trời ơi, sao lại thế này?
examples.translation: Oh my god, how did this happen?

natural: The very Northern (Hanoi) version of 'oh my god!'; drawn out for drama, often half-joking exasperation. 'giời' is the Northern colloquial pronunciation of 'trời'.
whoUses: Northern speakers, especially older women, often theatrically
usageContext: loud reaction to something outrageous, funny, or over-the-top.

examples.text: Ối giời ơi, đắt thế cơ à?
examples.translation: Oh my goodness, it's that expensive?!

natural: Intensifier meaning 'so / intensely'; can be good ('giỏi ghê' = so skilled) or bad ('sợ ghê' = so scary). Standalone 'ghê!' reacts to something impressive or gross.
usageContext: adding emphasis to an adjective, or reacting with awe/disgust.

examples.text: Con bé đó hát hay ghê.
examples.translation: That girl sings incredibly well.

natural: 'Living for the 'gram' / curating a fake online life'; posting glamorous photos that don't match real life. Mildly mocking.
usageContext: teasing someone who poses hard for photos or exaggerates their life online.

examples.text: Ra quán cà phê này sống ảo là chuẩn.
examples.translation: This cafe is perfect for taking Insta-worthy photos.

natural: 'Boyfriend / girlfriend / bae'; your romantic partner. To be single is 'chưa có gấu' (don't have a bear yet).
usageContext: referring playfully to one's significant other.

examples.text: Cuối tuần đi chơi với gấu à?
examples.translation: Going out with your bae this weekend?

natural: Borrowed English 'crush' - the person you secretly like. 'Thả thính' is the related slang for flirting to reel in a crush.
usageContext: talking about someone you have a one-sided crush on.

examples.text: Crush của tớ không rep tin nhắn.
examples.translation: My crush isn't replying to my messages.

natural: 'Sure, right now / done deal'; enthusiastic instant agreement. 'luôn' adds the 'immediately / no hesitation' punch.
usageContext: agreeing eagerly to a plan.

examples.text: Tối nay lẩu nhé? - Ok luôn!
examples.translation: Hotpot tonight? - Absolutely!

natural: 'Bored / boring / what a drag'; can describe your mood or a dull thing. 'Chán chết' (bored to death) is the amped-up version.
usageContext: complaining about boredom or a disappointing situation.

examples.text: Trời mưa suốt, chán thật.
examples.translation: It's been raining nonstop, what a drag.

natural: Literally 'tired', but colloquially also 'exhausting / such a hassle', as in 'mệt với anh' (you're exhausting to deal with).
usageContext: venting about tiredness or a person/thing that wears you out.

examples.text: Mệt với ông thật đấy!
examples.translation: You are seriously exhausting!

natural: Hugely versatile filler: 'enough! / stop it / never mind / alright then / let's just...'. Tone and context decide which. 'Thôi thôi' = 'okay okay, enough'.
usageContext: cutting something off, resigning to a decision, or softening the end of a sentence.

examples.text: Thôi, đừng nói nữa.
examples.translation: Enough, stop talking about it.

natural: 'Awkward / shy / can't be bothered'; embarrassment or reluctance. 'Ngại quá' = 'so awkward / I'm too shy'.
usageContext: declining out of shyness or being too lazy/embarrassed to do something.

examples.text: Ngại quá, tớ không dám hỏi đâu.
examples.translation: It's so awkward, I don't dare ask.

natural: 'I give up / I'm helpless / can't deal'; throwing your hands up at something impossible or absurd. '.com' is sometimes tacked on jokingly ('bó tay chấm com').
usageContext: reacting to something hopeless, ridiculous, or beyond your ability.

examples.text: Nó bướng thế thì tôi bó tay.
examples.translation: If he's that stubborn, I give up.

natural: 'Immature brat / edgy kid'; a young person (or anyone) who acts childish, cocky, or attention-seeking. Mildly derogatory.
usageContext: mocking immature online behavior or showing off.

examples.text: Mấy đứa trẻ trâu suốt ngày gây sự.
examples.translation: Those immature brats pick fights all day.

natural: 'Jelly / hating out of envy'; an acronym (Ghen Ăn Tức Ở, roughly 'jealous and resentful') that puns on the French word for cake. Half-joking.
usageContext: teasing someone for being jealous, or admitting your own envy playfully.

examples.text: Thấy tôi được thưởng là gato à?
examples.translation: Jealous that I got a bonus, huh?

phrase: Đứng núi này trông núi nọ
romaji: dung nui nay chong nui naw
literal: Standing on this mountain, gazing at that mountain
natural: 'The grass is always greener'; never satisfied with what you have, always eyeing something else.
usageContext: criticizing someone who is fickle or perpetually discontent.

examples.text: Làm việc gì cũng đứng núi này trông núi nọ thì chẳng đến đâu.
examples.translation: If you always want what you don't have, you'll get nowhere.

phrase: Có công mài sắt, có ngày nên kim
romaji: kaw kong mai sat, kaw ngai nen kim
literal: With effort grinding iron, one day it becomes a needle
natural: 'Persistence pays off / patience and hard work bring success'; a classic encouragement proverb.
usageContext: encouraging someone to keep trying through difficulty.

examples.text: Cứ chăm chỉ đi, có công mài sắt có ngày nên kim.
examples.translation: Just keep working hard - persistence pays off.
